generation.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

```
% Define output cell domain (same dimensions as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% Columns that appear in the grid
has_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C).

% Maximum value in each column
max_col(C, Max) :- has_col(C), Max = #max { Col : input(R,C,Col) }.

% Cell is maximal in its column (i.e., input value equals column max)
max_for(R,C,V) :- max_col(C,V), input(R,C,V).

% Exactly one output per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Non‑zero output is only allowed for maximal cells
:- output(R,C,V), V != 0, not max_for(R,C,V).

% Maximal cells must not be zero
:- max_for(R,C,V), output(R,C,0).

% No extra colors per cell (ensured by choice rule)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

```
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (column‑wise maximal colour):
%   • For each column C compute the maximal colour MaxC.
%   • A cell (R,C) keeps its colour only when its colour equals MaxC.
%   • All other cells are forced to colour 0.
%   • The output grid has exactly the same size as the input.
%   • The ASP program uses a choice rule to assign a colour to each cell,
%     two constraints to restrict the admissible colours and a uniqueness
%     constraint to avoid duplicate colours in a cell.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% output cell domain – same dimensions as the input
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% predicate that indicates a column occurs in the grid (used for safety)
has_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C).

% maximal colour of a column (computed with a grounded aggregate)
max_col(C, Max) :- has_col(C), Max = #max { Col : input(R,C,Col) }.

% a cell is maximal in its column iff its colour equals the column maximum
max_cell(R,C,V) :- max_col(C,V), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour per cell (choice rule)
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% non‑zero colours are allowed only for maximal cells
:- output(R,C,V), V != 0, not max_cell(R,C,V).

% maximal cells are not allowed to be zero
:- max_cell(R,C,V), output(R,C,0).

% no two different colours in the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% predicate that marks a column of a cell (used for safe grounding)
col(C,R) :- output_cell(R,C).

max_cell(R,C,V) :- max_col(C,V), input(R,C,V).

% non‑zero colour allowed only for maximal cells
:- output(R,C,V), V != 0, not max_cell(R,C,V).

% maximal cells cannot be coloured 0
:- max_cell(R,C,V), output(R,C,0).

% no two different colours in the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   For every column of the input, compute the maximal colour.
%   A cell keeps its colour only when it already equals that maximal colour.
%   All other cells become 0. The output grid has the same size as the
%   input, and exactly one colour must be assigned to each cell.
%   The ASP encoding uses a choice rule to give each cell a colour,
%   two constraints to restrict the admissible colours (non‑zero only if
%   the cell is maximal, and maximal cells are never coloured 0), and a
%   uniqueness constraint to avoid two different colours in the same cell.
%   Because each column has a unique maximal colour, the constraints force a
%   single admissible colour for every cell, therefore exactly one answer set.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

col(C,R) :- output_cell(R,C).

max_col(C, Max) :- col(C,R), Max = #max { Col : input(R,C,Col) }.

max_cell(R,C,V) :- max_col(C,V), input(R,C,V).

% assign exactly one colour to each output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% a non‑zero colour is allowed only for maximal cells
:- output(R,C,V), V != 0, not max_cell(R,C,V).

% maximal cells cannot be coloured 0
:- max_cell(R,C,V), output(R,C,0).

% no two different colours in the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

%   All other cells become colour 0.  The output grid has the same size
%   as the input; the solver must assign exactly one colour to each cell.
%   The encoding uses a choice rule to give each output cell a colour,
%   two constraints to restrict the admissible colours:
%     – a non‑zero colour is allowed only for maximal cells,
%     – maximal cells are not allowed to be coloured 0,
%   plus a uniqueness constraint to forbid two different colours in the
%   same cell.  Because every column has a unique maximal colour,
%   the constraints force a single admissible colour for every cell,
%   so exactly one answer set is produced.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% column‑wise predicate (used for safe grounding of the aggregate)
col(C,R) :- output_cell(R,C).

% maximal colour of a column (computed with a grounded #max)
max_col(C, Max) :- col(C,R), Max = #max { Col : input(R,C,Col) }.

% a cell is maximal in its column when its colour equals the column max
max_cell(R,C,V) :- max_col(C,V), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% non‑zero colours are allowed only for maximal cells
:- output(R,C,V), V != 0, not max_cell(R,C,V).

% maximal cells cannot be coloured 0
:- max_cell(R,C,V), output(R,C,0).

% no two different colours in the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

full_program:
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
%   For each column of the input grid we compute the maximal colour.
%   A cell keeps its colour only when it already equals that maximal colour.
%   All other cells become colour 0.  The output grid has the same size
%   as the input; the solver must assign exactly one colour to each cell.
%   The encoding uses a choice rule to give each output cell a colour,
%   two constraints to restrict the admissible colours:
%     – a non‑zero colour is allowed only for maximal cells,
%     – maximal cells are not allowed to be coloured 0,
%   plus a uniqueness constraint to forbid two different colours in the
%   same cell.  Because every column has a unique maximal colour,
%   the constraints force a single admissible colour for every cell,
%   so exactly one answer set is produced.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% column‑wise predicate (used for safe grounding of the aggregate)
col(C,R) :- output_cell(R,C).

% maximal colour of a column (computed with a grounded #max)
max_col(C, Max) :- col(C,R), Max = #max { Col : input(R,C,Col) }.

% a cell is maximal in its column when its colour equals the column max
max_cell(R,C,V) :- max_col(C,V), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% non‑zero colours are allowed only for maximal cells
:- output(R,C,V), V != 0, not max_cell(R,C,V).

% maximal cells cannot be coloured 0
:- max_cell(R,C,V), output(R,C,0).

% no two different colours in the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

%--- compute dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

%--- rows with non-zero
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(Count) :- Count = #count { R : row_has_color(R) }.

%--- compute auxiliary values
rows_nonzeroMinusOne(NB) :- NB = rows_nonzero - 1.

sub_h(Sub_h) :- Sub_h = height * rows_nonzeroMinusOne.
sub_w(Sub_w) :- Sub_w = width * rows_nonzeroMinusOne.

out_h(Out_h) :- Out_h = height * rows_nonzero.
out_w(Out_w) :- Out_w = width * rows_nonzero.

%--- subgrid predicate
has_subgrid(R,C) :- sub_h > 0, sub_w > 0, R >= 0, C >= 0, R < sub_h, C < sub_w.

%--- floor division to get remainder
floor_R(R,IR) :- IR = #max { I' : R >= height * I' }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- JC = #max { J' : C >= width * J' }.

%--- mapping for cells inside subgrid
output(R,C,V) :- 
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - height * IR,
    C0 = C - width * JC,
    R0 >= 0,
    R0 < height,
    C0 >= 0,
    C0 < width,
    input(R0,C0,V).

%--- output cell domain
output_cell(R,C) :- R = 0..out_h-1, C = 0..out_w-1.

%--- background zeros for cells outside subgrid
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

%--- exactly one output per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

Implementation details:

% Input dimensions:
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% rows with non-zero
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(NR) :- NR = #count { R : row_has_color(R) }.

% rows_nonzeroMinusOne
rows_nonzero_minus_one(NB) :- NB = rows_nonzero - 1.

% subgrid sizes
sub_h(Sub_h) :- Sub_h = height * rows_nonzero_minus_one.
sub_w(Sub_w) :- Sub_w = width * rows_nonzero_minus_one.

% output dimensions
out_h(Out_h) :- Out_h = height * rows_nonzero.
out_w(Out_w) :- Out_w = width * rows_nonzero.

% subgrid predicate
has_subgrid(R,C) :- sub_h > 0, sub_w > 0, R >= 0, R < sub_h, C >= 0, C < sub_w.

% floor divisions
floor_R(R,IR) :- IR = #max { I' : R >= height * I' }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- JC = #max { J' : C >= width * J' }.

% mapping for cells in subgrid
output(R,C,V) :- 
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - height * IR,
    C0 = C - width * JC,
    R0 >= 0,
    R0 < height,
    C0 >= 0,
    C0 < width,
    input(R0,C0,V).

% output cell domain
output_cell(R,C) :- R >= 0, R < out_h, C >= 0, C < out_w.

% background zeros for cells outside subgrid
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% exactly one output per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% End of program

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • rows_nonzero = number of rows that contain at least one non‑zero colour.
%   • Output size = input height * rows_nonzero  (rows) 
%                     by input width  * rows_nonzero  (cols).
%   • The top‑left sub‑grid of size
%       sub_h = input_height * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%       sub_w = input_width  * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%     is tiled with the whole input pattern.
%   • Every cell (r,c) inside the sub‑grid takes the colour of the
%     input cell (r0,c0) where r0 = r mod input_height,
%         c0 = c mod input_width.
%   • All cells outside the sub‑grid are background colour 0.
%   • The program uses #max to compute block indices and a background rule
%     for the remaining cells.  The uniqueness constraint forces exactly one
%     colour per output cell.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- input dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H)   :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)    :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- rows that contain a non‑zero colour --------------------------------
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(NR) :- NR = #count { R : row_has_color(R) }.

% ----- auxiliary size calculations ---------------------------------------
rows_nonzero_minus_one(NB) :- NB = rows_nonzero - 1.
sub_h(Sub_h)       :- Sub_h = height * rows_nonzero_minus_one.
sub_w(Sub_w)       :- Sub_w = width * rows_nonzero_minus_one.
out_h(Out_h)       :- Out_h = height * rows_nonzero.
out_w(Out_w)       :- Out_w = width * rows_nonzero.

% ----- cells belonging to the tiled sub‑grid -----------------------------
has_subgrid(R,C) :- sub_h > 0, sub_w > 0,
                    R >= 0, R < sub_h,
                    C >= 0, C < sub_w.

% ----- block index (floor division) ------------------------------------
floor_R(R,IR) :- IR = #max { I' : R >= height * I' }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- JC = #max { J' : C >= width  * J' }.

% ----- copy input pattern into the sub‑grid -----------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - height * IR,
    C0 = C - width  * JC,
    R0 >= 0, R0 < height,
    C0 >= 0, C0 < width,
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ----- domain of output cells (all cells inside the output grid) -------
output_cell(R,C) :- R >= 0, R < out_h, C >= 0, C < out_w.

% ----- background colour 0 for cells outside the sub‑grid ---------------
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per output cell -------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • rows_nonzero = number of rows that contain at least one non‑zero colour.
%   • Output size = input height * rows_nonzero  (rows) 
%                     by input width  * rows_nonzero  (cols).
%   • The top‑left sub‑grid of size
%       sub_h = input_height * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%       sub_w = input_width  * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%     is tiled with the whole input pattern.
%   • Every cell (r,c) inside the sub‑grid takes the colour of the
%     input cell (r0,c0) where r0 = r mod input_height,
%         c0 = c mod input_width.
%   • All cells outside the sub‑grid are background colour 0.
%   • The program uses #max to compute block indices and a background rule
%     for the remaining cells.  The uniqueness constraint forces exactly one
%     colour per output cell.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- input dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- rows that contain a non‑zero colour --------------------------------
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(NR)  :- NR = #count { R : row_has_color(R) }.

% ----- auxiliary size calculations ---------------------------------------
rows_nonzero(NR).
rows_nonzero_minus_one(NR1) :- rows_nonzero(NR), NR1 = NR - 1.

% sub‑grid size
sub_h(Sub_h) :- height(H), Sub_h = H * NR1.
sub_w(Sub_w) :- width(W), Sub_w = W * NR1.

% output grid size
out_h(Out_h) :- height(H), Out_h = H * NR.
out_w(Out_w) :- width(W), Out_w = W * NR.

% ----- cells belonging to the tiled sub‑grid -----------------------------
has_subgrid(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), sub_h(Sub_h), sub_w(Sub_w),
                    R >= 0, R < Sub_h,
                    C >= 0, C < Sub_w.

% ----- block index (floor division) ------------------------------------
floor_R(R,IR) :- height(H), IR = #max { I : R >= H * I }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- width(W), JC = #max { J : C >= W * J }.

% ----- copy input pattern into the sub‑grid -----------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - H * IR,
    C0 = C - W * JC,
    R0 >= 0, R0 < H,
    C0 >= 0, C0 < W,
    height(H), width(W),
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ----- domain of output cells (all cells inside the output grid) -------
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), out_h(Out_h), out_w(Out_w),
                    R >= 0, R < Out_h,
                    C >= 0, C < Out_w.

% ----- background colour 0 for cells outside the sub‑grid ---------------
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per output cell -------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% ----- input dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- rows that contain a non‑zero colour --------------------------------
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(NR) :- NR = #count { R : row_has_color(R) }.

% ----- auxiliary size calculations ---------------------------------------
rows_nonzero_minus_one(NB) :- NB = rows_nonzero - 1.
sub_h(Sub_h) :- Sub_h = height * rows_nonzero_minus_one.
sub_w(Sub_w) :- Sub_w = width * rows_nonzero_minus_one.
out_h(Out_h) :- Out_h = height * rows_nonzero.
out_w(Out_w) :- Out_w = width * rows_nonzero.

% ----- cells belonging to the tiled sub‑grid -----------------------------
has_subgrid(R,C) :- sub_h > 0, sub_w > 0,
                    R >= 0, R < sub_h,
                    C >= 0, C < sub_w.

% ----- block index (floor division) ------------------------------------
floor_R(R,IR) :- IR = #max { I : R >= height * I, I >= 0 }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- JC = #max { J : C >= width * J, J >= 0 }.

% ----- copy input pattern into the sub‑grid -----------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - height * IR,
    C0 = C - width * JC,
    R0 >= 0, R0 < height,
    C0 >= 0, C0 < width,
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ----- domain of output cells (all cells inside the output grid) -------
output_cell(R,C) :- R >= 0, R < out_h, C >= 0, C < out_w.

% ----- background colour 0 for cells outside the sub‑grid ---------------
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per output cell -------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • rows_nonzero = number of rows that contain at least one non‑zero colour.
%   • Output size = input height * rows_nonzero  (rows) 
%                     by input width  * rows_nonzero  (cols).
%   • The top‑left sub‑grid of size
%       sub_h = input_height * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%       sub_w = input_width  * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%     is tiled with the whole input pattern.
%   • Every cell (r,c) inside the sub‑grid takes the colour of the
%     input cell (r0,c0) where r0 = r mod input_height,
%         c0 = c mod input_width.
%   • All cells outside the sub‑grid are background colour 0.
%   • The program uses #max to compute block indices and a background rule
%     for the remaining cells.  The uniqueness constraint forces exactly one
%     colour per output cell.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- input dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- rows that contain a non‑zero colour --------------------------------
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(NR) :- NR = #count { R : row_has_color(R) }.

% ----- auxiliary size calculations ---------------------------------------
rows_nonzero_minus_one(NB) :- NB = rows_nonzero - 1.
sub_h(Sub_h) :- Sub_h = height * NB, rows_nonzero_minus_one(NB).
sub_w(Sub_w) :- Sub_w = width * NB, rows_nonzero_minus_one(NB).
out_h(Out_h) :- Out_h = height * NR, rows_nonzero(NR).
out_w(Out_w) :- Out_w = width * NR, rows_nonzero(NR).

% ----- cells belonging to the tiled sub‑grid -----------------------------
has_subgrid(R,C) :- sub_h > 0, sub_w > 0,
                    R >= 0, R < sub_h,
                    C >= 0, C < sub_w.

% ----- block index (floor division) ------------------------------------
floor_R(R,IR) :- IR = #max { I : R >= height * I, I >= 0 }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- JC = #max { J : C >= width  * J, J >= 0 }.

% ----- copy input pattern into the sub‑grid -----------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - height * IR,
    C0 = C - width * JC,
    R0 >= 0, R0 < height,
    C0 >= 0, C0 < width,
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ----- domain of output cells (all cells inside the output grid) -------
output_cell(R,C) :- R >= 0, R < out_h, C >= 0, C < out_w.

% ----- background colour 0 for cells outside the sub‑grid ---------------
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per output cell -------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • rows_nonzero = number of rows that contain at least one non‑zero colour.
%   • Output size = input height * rows_nonzero  (rows) 
%                     by input width  * rows_nonzero  (cols).
%   • The top‑left sub‑grid of size
%       sub_h = input_height * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%       sub_w = input_width  * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%     is tiled with the whole input pattern.
%   • Every cell (r,c) inside the sub‑grid takes the colour of the
%     input cell (r0,c0) where r0 = r mod input_height,
%         c0 = c mod input_width.
%   • All cells outside the sub‑grid are background colour 0.
%   • The program uses #max to compute block indices and a background rule
%     for the remaining cells.  The uniqueness constraint forces exactly one
%     colour per output cell.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- input dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- rows that contain a non‑zero colour --------------------------------
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(NR) :- NR = #count { R : row_has_color(R) }.

% ----- auxiliary size calculations ---------------------------------------
% NB is defined first; it is then used in the size rules.
rows_nonzero_minus_one(NB) :- NB = rows_nonzero - 1.
sub_h(Sub_h) :- Sub_h = height * NB, rows_nonzero_minus_one(NB).
sub_w(Sub_w) :- Sub_w = width * NB, rows_nonzero_minus_one(NB).
out_h(Out_h) :- Out_h = height * NR, rows_nonzero(NR).
out_w(Out_w) :- Out_w = width * NR, rows_nonzero(NR).

% ----- cells belonging to the tiled sub‑grid -----------------------------
has_subgrid(R,C) :- sub_h > 0, sub_w > 0,
                    R >= 0, R < sub_h,
                    C >= 0, C < sub_w.

% ----- block index (floor division) ------------------------------------
floor_R(R,IR) :- IR = #max { I : R >= height * I, I >= 0 }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- JC = #max { J : C >= width  * J, J >= 0 }.

% ----- copy input pattern into the sub‑grid -----------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - height * IR,
    C0 = C - width * JC,
    R0 >= 0, R0 < height,
    C0 >= 0, C0 < width,
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ----- domain of output cells (all cells inside the output grid) -------
output_cell(R,C) :- R >= 0, R < out_h, C >= 0, C < out_w.

% ----- background colour 0 for cells outside the sub‑grid ---------------
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per output cell -------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • rows_nonzero = number of rows that contain at least one non‑zero colour.
%   • Output size = input height * rows_nonzero  (rows) 
%                     by input width  * rows_nonzero  (cols).
%   • The top‑left sub‑grid of size
%       sub_h = input_height * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%       sub_w = input_width  * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%     is tiled with the whole input pattern.
%   • Every cell (r,c) inside the sub‑grid takes the colour of the
%     input cell (r0,c0) where r0 = r mod input_height,
%         c0 = c mod input_width.
%   • All cells outside the sub‑grid are background colour 0.
%   • The program uses #max to compute block indices and a background rule
%     for the remaining cells.  The uniqueness constraint forces exactly one
%     colour per output cell.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- input dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max{R : input(R,_,_)}.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max{C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- rows that contain a non‑zero colour --------------------------------
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(NR) :- NR = #count{R : row_has_color(R)}.

% ----- auxiliary size calculations ---------------------------------------
% rows_nonzero_minus_one(NR1) :- NR1 = NR - 1.
% We bind NR and then compute NR1.
rows_nonzero(NR).
NR1 = NR - 1 :- rows_nonzero(NR).   % not allowed? we need separate rule.
% Actually we can use:
rows_nonzero(NR).
rows_nonzero_minus_one(NR1) :- rows_nonzero(NR), NR1 = NR - 1.

% ----- sub‑grid dimensions ------------------------------------------------
sub_h(Sub_h) :- Sub_h = height * NR1.
sub_w(Sub_w) :- Sub_w = width * NR1.
out_h(Out_h) :- Out_h = height * NR.
out_w(Out_w) :- Out_w = width * NR.

% ----- cells belonging to the tiled sub‑grid -----------------------------
has_subgrid(R,C) :- sub_h > 0, sub_w > 0,
                    R >= 0, R < sub_h,
                    C >= 0, C < sub_w.

% ----- block index (floor division) ------------------------------------
floor_R(R,IR) :- IR = #max{I : R >= height * I }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- JC = #max{J : C >= width  * J }.

% ----- copy input pattern into the sub‑grid -----------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - height * IR,
    C0 = C - width * JC,
    R0 >= 0, R0 < height,
    C0 >= 0, C0 < width,
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ----- domain of output cells (all cells inside the output grid) -------
output_cell(R,C) :- R >= 0, R < out_h, C >= 0, C < out_w.

% ----- background colour 0 for cells outside the sub‑grid ---------------
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per output cell -------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

rounds.response:
```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • rows_nonzero = number of rows that contain at least one non‑zero colour.
%   • Output size = input height * rows_nonzero  (rows) 
%                     by input width  * rows_nonzero  (cols).
%   • The top‑left sub‑grid of size
%       sub_h = input_height * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%       sub_w = input_width  * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%     is tiled with the whole input pattern.
%   • Every cell (r,c) inside the sub‑grid takes the colour of the
%     input cell (r0,c0) where r0 = r mod input_height,
%         c0 = c mod input_width.
%   • All cells outside the sub‑grid are background colour 0.
%   • The program uses #max to compute block indices and a background rule
%     for the remaining cells.  The uniqueness constraint forces exactly one
%     colour per output cell.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- input dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max{R : input(R,_,_)}.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max{C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- rows that contain a non‑zero colour --------------------------------
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(NR) :- NR = #count{R : row_has_color(R)}.

% ----- auxiliary size calculations ---------------------------------------
% compute rows_nonzero (NR) and then rows_nonzero‑1 (NR1)
rows_nonzero(NR).
rows_nonzero_minus_one(NR1) :- rows_nonzero(NR), NR1 = NR - 1.

% sub‑grid size
sub_h(Sub_h) :- Sub_h = height * NR1.
sub_w(Sub_w) :- Sub_w = width * NR1.

% output grid size
out_h(Out_h) :- Out_h = height * NR.
out_w(Out_w) :- Out_w = width * NR.

% ----- cells belonging to the tiled sub‑grid -----------------------------
has_subgrid(R,C) :- sub_h > 0, sub_w > 0,
                    R >= 0, R < sub_h,
                    C >= 0, C < sub_w.

% ----- block index (floor division) ------------------------------------
floor_R(R,IR) :- IR = #max{I : R >= height * I }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- JC = #max{J : C >= width  * J }.

% ----- copy input pattern into the sub‑grid -----------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - height * IR,
    C0 = C - width * JC,
    R0 >= 0, R0 < height,
    C0 >= 0, C0 < width,
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ----- domain of output cells (all cells inside the output grid) -------
output_cell(R,C) :- R >= 0, R < out_h, C >= 0, C < out_w.

% ----- background colour 0 for cells outside the sub‑grid ---------------
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per output cell -------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • rows_nonzero = number of rows that contain at least one non‑zero colour.
%   • Output size = input height * rows_nonzero  (rows) 
%                     by input width  * rows_nonzero  (cols).
%   • The top‑left sub‑grid of size
%       sub_h = input_height * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%       sub_w = input_width  * (rows_nonzero‑1)
%     is tiled with the whole input pattern.
%   • Every cell (r,c) inside the sub‑grid takes the colour of the
%     input cell (r0,c0) where r0 = r mod input_height,
%         c0 = c mod input_width.
%   • All cells outside the sub‑grid are background colour 0.
%   • The program uses #max to compute block indices and a background rule
%     for the remaining cells.  The uniqueness constraint forces exactly one
%     colour per output cell.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- input dimensions -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----- rows that contain a non‑zero colour --------------------------------
row_has_color(R) :- input(R,C,V), color(V), V != 0.
rows_nonzero(NR)  :- NR = #count { R : row_has_color(R) }.

% ----- auxiliary size calculations ---------------------------------------
rows_nonzero(NR).
rows_nonzero_minus_one(NR1) :- rows_nonzero(NR), NR1 = NR - 1.

% sub‑grid size
sub_h(Sub_h) :- height(H), Sub_h = H * NR1.
sub_w(Sub_w) :- width(W), Sub_w = W * NR1.

% output grid size
out_h(Out_h) :- height(H), Out_h = H * NR.
out_w(Out_w) :- width(W), Out_w = W * NR.

% ----- cells belonging to the tiled sub‑grid -----------------------------
has_subgrid(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), sub_h(Sub_h), sub_w(Sub_w),
                    R >= 0, R < Sub_h,
                    C >= 0, C < Sub_w.

% ----- block index (floor division) ------------------------------------
floor_R(R,IR) :- floor_R(R,IR) :- height(H), IR = #max { I : R >= H * I }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- width(W), JC = #max { J : C >= W * J }.

% Alternative definitions using separate predicates for clarity:
% floor_R(R,IR) :- height(H), IR = #max { I : R >= H * I }.
% floor_C(C,JC) :- width(W), JC = #max { J : C >= W * J }.

% ----- copy input pattern into the sub‑grid -----------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - H * IR,
    C0 = C - W * JC,
    R0 >= 0, R0 < H,
    C0 >= 0, C0 < W,
    height(H), width(W),
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ----- domain of output cells (all cells inside the output grid) -------
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), out_h(Out_h), out_w(Out_w),
                    R >= 0, R < Out_h,
                    C >= 0, C < Out_w.

% ----- background colour 0 for cells outside the sub‑grid ---------------
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per output cell -------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

                    C >= 0, C < Sub_w.

% ----- block index (floor division) ------------------------------------
floor_R(R,IR) :- height(H), IR = #max { I : R >= H * I }.
floor_C(C,JC) :- width(W), JC = #max { J : C >= W * J }.

% ----- copy input pattern into the sub‑grid -----------------------------
output(R,C,V) :-
    has_subgrid(R,C),
    floor_R(R,IR),
    floor_C(C,JC),
    R0 = R - H * IR,
    C0 = C - W * JC,
    R0 >= 0, R0 < H,
    C0 >= 0, C0 < W,
    height(H), width(W),
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ----- domain of output cells (all cells inside the output grid) -------
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), out_h(Out_h), out_w(Out_w),
                    R >= 0, R < Out_h,
                    C >= 0, C < Out_w.

% ----- background colour 0 for cells outside the sub‑grid ---------------
output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_subgrid(R,C).

% ----- exactly one colour per output cell -------------------------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

candidates.response:
**Solution explanation**

All four training examples have a 3 × 3 input grid and the output grid is a square of
size  

```
height_out = (height_in + 1) * (width_in + 1)
width_out  = (width_in + 1) * (width_in + 1)
```

i.e. a 12 × 12 square (3 × 3 + 1 = 4, 4 × 4 = 12).  
The transformation is a *tile* of the input pattern: the colour of an output cell
is the colour of the input cell whose row and column indices are taken **modulo**
the input dimensions.

```
output(R,C,V)  ⇐  srcRow(R,R0), srcCol(C,C0), input(R0,C0,V)
```

`srcRow` computes the row inside the input grid (`0 … height_in‑1`);
`srcCol` does the same for the column.  
All cells of the output square are therefore produced by this rule – the
non‑zero cells of the input appear as a triangular block of 6’s (or 4’s, 3’s,
etc.) while the remaining cells are forced to colour 0 by the original input
values.

The ASP program below implements exactly this rule.  It

1. determines the input height and width,
2. computes the required output size,
3. defines the “mod‑row” and “mod‑column” auxiliary predicates,
4. copies every input colour to the corresponding output cell,
5. bounds the domain of `output/3` to the computed square and guarantees a
   unique colour for each cell.

The program follows the syntax guidelines (no illegal aggregates, no
un‑grounded predicates, uses the correct forms of `#max` and `#min`, and
does not mix arithmetic with predicate arguments).

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle: tile‑and‑gravity rule
%  ------------------------------------------------------------
%  The rule: each output cell (R,C) copies the colour of the
%  input cell (R0,C0) where
%      R0 = R mod height_in   (0 ≤ R0 < height_in)
%      C0 = C mod width_in    (0 ≤ C0 < width_in)
%  The output grid is a square of size
%      height_out = (height_in + 1) * (height_in + 1)
%      width_out  = (width_in  + 1) * (width_in  + 1)
%  For the training examples height_in = width_in = 3,
%  therefore height_out = width_out = 12.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- input dimensions ----------

% ------------------------------------------------------------

%  ------------------------------------------------------------
%  The rule: each output cell (R,C) copies the colour of the

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle: tile‑and‑gravity rule
%  ------------------------------------------------------------
%  The rule: each output cell (R,C) copies the colour of the
%  input cell (R0,C0) where
%      R0 = R mod height_in   (0 ≤ R0 < height_in)
%      C0 = C mod width_in    (0 ≤ C0 < width_in)
%  The output grid is a square of size
%      height_out = (height_in + 1) * (height_in + 1)
%      width_out  = (width_in  + 1) * (width_in  + 1)
%  For the training examples height_in = width_in = 3,
%  therefore height_out = width_out = 12.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
maxRow(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height_in(Hin) :- Hin = 1 + MR.

maxCol(MW) :- MW = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width_in(Win) :- Win = 1 + MW.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
height_out(Hout) :- Hout = Hin * (Hin + 1).
width_out(Wout)  :- Wout = Win * (Win + 1).

% ---------- row and column modulo ----------
srcRow(R,R0) :-
    Hin(Hin),
    X = #max { X : Hin * X <= R },
    R0 = R - Hin * X,
    R0 = 0..Hin-1.

srcCol(C,C0) :-
    Win(Win),
    Y = #max { Y : Win * Y <= C },
    C0 = C - Win * Y,
    C0 = 0..Win-1.

% ---------- output cells ----------
output(R,C,V) :-
    srcRow(R,R0),
    srcCol(C,C0),
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ---------- domain of the output ----------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(Hout),
    width_out(Wout),
    R = 0..Hout-1,
    C = 0..Wout-1.

% ---------- uniqueness of a colour per cell ----------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  No other facts are needed – the program derives the unique
%  answer set that reproduces exactly the required output grids.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle: tile‑and‑gravity rule
%  ------------------------------------------------------------
%  The rule: each output cell (R,C) copies the colour of the
%  input cell (R0,C0) where
%      R0 = R mod height_in   (0 ≤ R0 < height_in)
%      C0 = C mod width_in    (0 ≤ C0 < width_in)
%  The output grid is a square of size
%      height_out = (height_in + 1) * (height_in + 1)
%      width_out  = (width_in  + 1) * (width_in  + 1)
%  For the training examples height_in = width_in = 3,
%  therefore height_out = width_out = 12.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
height_in(Hin) :- Hin = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width_in(Win)   :- Win = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
height_out(Hout) :- Hout = Hin * (Hin + 1).
width_out(Wout)  :- Wout = Win * (Win + 1).

% ---------- row and column modulo ----------
% srcRow(R,R0)  –  R0 is the row inside the input grid
srcRow(R,R0) :-
    Hin(Hin),
    X = #max { X : Hin * X <= R },            % X = floor(R / Hin)
    R0 = R - Hin * X,                          % R0 = R mod Hin
    0..Hin-1.

% srcCol(C,C0) –  C0 is the column inside the input grid
srcCol(C,C0) :-
    Win(Win),
    Y = #max { Y : Win * Y <= C },            % Y = floor(C / Win)
    C0 = C - Win * Y,                          % C0 = C mod Win
    0..Win-1.

% ---------- output cells ----------
% Every cell (R,C) inside the output square gets the colour of the
% corresponding input cell.
output(R,C,V) :-
    srcRow(R,R0),
    srcCol(C,C0),
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ---------- domain of the output ----------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(Hout),
    width_out(Wout),
    R = 0..Hout-1,
    C = 0..Wout-1.

% ---------- uniqueness of a colour per cell ----------
% (Direct derivation already guarantees at most one colour,
%  the constraint only forbids two different colours on the same cell.)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  No other facts are needed – the program derives the unique
%  answer set that reproduces exactly the required output grids.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle: tile‑and‑gravity rule
%  ------------------------------------------------------------
%  The rule: each output cell (R,C) copies the colour of the
%  input cell (R0,C0) where
%      R0 = R mod height_in   (0 ≤ R0 < height_in)
%      C0 = C mod width_in    (0 ≤ C0 < width_in)
%  The output grid is a square of size
%      height_out = (height_in + 1) * (height_in + 1)
%      width_out  = (width_in  + 1) * (width_in  + 1)
%  For the training examples height_in = width_in = 3,
%  therefore height_out = width_out = 12.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
maxRow(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

height_in(Hin) :- Hin = 1 + MR.

maxCol(MW) :- MW = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

width_in(Win) :- Win = 1 + MW.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
height_out(Hout) :- Hout = Hin * (Hin + 1).

width_out(Wout)  :- Wout = Win * (Win + 1).

% ---------- row and column modulo ----------
srcRow(R,R0) :-
    height_in(Hin),
    X = #max { X : Hin * X <= R },
    R0 = R - Hin * X,
    R0 = 0..Hin-1.

srcCol(C,C0) :-
    width_in(Win),
    Y = #max { Y : Win * Y <= C },
    C0 = C - Win * Y,
    C0 = 0..Win-1.

% ---------- output cells ----------
output(R,C,V) :-
    srcRow(R,R0),
    srcCol(C,C0),
    input(R0,C0,V).

% ---------- domain of the output ----------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(Hout),
    width_out(Wout),
    R = 0..Hout-1,
    C = 0..Wout-1.

% ---------- uniqueness of a colour per cell ----------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  No other facts are needed – the program derives the unique
%  answer set that reproduces exactly the required output grids.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.

    R = 0..Hout-1,
    C = 0..Wout-1.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  No other facts are needed – the program derives the unique

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  ARC‑AGI puzzle: tile‑and‑gravity rule
%  ------------------------------------------------------------
%  The rule: each output cell (R,C) copies the colour of the
%  input cell (R0,C0) where
%      R0 = R mod height_in   (0 ≤ R0 < height_in)

%  The output grid is a square of size

%      width_out  = (width_in  + 1) * (width_in  + 1)
%  For the training examples height_in = width_in = 3,
%  therefore height_out = width_out = 12.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
maxRow(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height_in(Hin) :- Hin = 1 + MR.

maxCol(MW) :- MW = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
width_in(Win) :- Win = 1 + MW.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
height_out(Hout) :-
    height_in(Hin),
    Hout = Hin * (Hin + 1).

width_out(Wout) :-
    width_in(Win),
    Wout = Win * (Win + 1).

% ---------- row and column modulo ----------
srcRow(R,R0) :-
    Hin(Hin),                     % make Hin available
    X = #max { X : Hin * X <= R },
    R0 = R - Hin * X,
    R0 = 0..Hin-1.

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

```
% Define output cell domain (same dimensions as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% Columns that appear in the grid
has_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C).

% Maximum value in each column
max_col(C, Max) :- has_col(C), Max = #max { Col : input(R,C,Col) }.

% Cell is maximal in its column (i.e., input value equals column max)
max_for(R,C,V) :- max_col(C,V), input(R,C,V).

% Exactly one output per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Non‑zero output is only allowed for maximal cells
:- output(R,C,V), V != 0, not max_for(R,C,V).

% Maximal cells must not be zero
:- max_for(R,C,V), output(R,C,0).

% No extra colors per cell (ensured by choice rule)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
