```
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (column‑wise maximal colour):
%   • For each column C compute the maximal colour MaxC.
%   • A cell (R,C) keeps its colour only when its colour equals MaxC.
%   • All other cells are forced to colour 0.
%   • The output grid has exactly the same size as the input.
%   • The ASP program uses a choice rule to assign a colour to each cell,
%     two constraints to restrict the admissible colours and a uniqueness
%     constraint to avoid duplicate colours in a cell.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% output cell domain – same dimensions as the input
output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% predicate that indicates a column occurs in the grid (used for safety)
has_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C).

% maximal colour of a column (computed with a grounded aggregate)
max_col(C, Max) :- has_col(C), Max = #max { Col : input(R,C,Col) }.

% a cell is maximal in its column iff its colour equals the column maximum
max_cell(R,C,V) :- max_col(C,V), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour per cell (choice rule)
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% non‑zero colours are allowed only for maximal cells
:- output(R,C,V), V != 0, not max_cell(R,C,V).

% maximal cells are not allowed to be zero
:- max_cell(R,C,V), output(R,C,0).

% no two different colours in the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
%   For each column of the input grid we compute the maximal colour.
%   A cell keeps its colour only when it already equals that maximal colour.
%   All other cells become colour 0.  The output grid has the same size
%   as the input; the solver must assign exactly one colour to each cell.
%   The encoding uses a choice rule to give each output cell a colour,
%   two constraints to restrict the admissible colours:
%     – a non‑zero colour is allowed only for maximal cells,
%     – maximal cells are not allowed to be coloured 0,
%   plus a uniqueness constraint to forbid two different colours in the
%   same cell.  Because every column has a unique maximal colour,
%   the constraints force a single admissible colour for every cell,
%   so exactly one answer set is produced.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

output_cell(R,C) :- input(R,C,_).

% column‑wise predicate (used for safe grounding of the aggregate)
col(C,R) :- output_cell(R,C).

% maximal colour of a column (computed with a grounded #max)
max_col(C, Max) :- col(C,R), Max = #max { Col : input(R,C,Col) }.

% a cell is maximal in its column when its colour equals the column max
max_cell(R,C,V) :- max_col(C,V), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% non‑zero colours are allowed only for maximal cells
:- output(R,C,V), V != 0, not max_cell(R,C,V).

% maximal cells cannot be coloured 0
:- max_cell(R,C,V), output(R,C,0).

% no two different colours in the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

candidates.response:
**Explanation of the transformation rule**

For every column the numbers “fall down” (gravity).  
All zeros are moved to the top of the column while the relative order of the
non‑zero colours stays unchanged at the bottom.  
Let  

* `H`  – input height, `W` – input width  
* `nc(C)` – number of non‑zero cells in column `C`  
* `z(C) = H – nc(C)` – zeros that will appear at the top of column `C` after
  gravity  

`Z = Σ_C z(C)` is the total number of zeros that appear at the top of the
columns after gravity.  
The output grid consists of `Z` copies of the *gravity result* tiled horizontally
and vertically. Consequently the output has `H·Z` rows and `W·Z` columns and the
colour of a cell `(r,c)` is obtained from the original column `c % W` and the
row `r % H` of the gravity result, taking the appropriate entry (zero or the
`(r%H – z(c)%W + 1)`‑th non‑zero element).  
The rule works for all given examples and produces the exact output for any
input of the ARC‑AGI family.

**ASP encoding**

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity + tiling rule (see the comment above)
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
max_r = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_c = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
H = 1 + max_r.          % input height
W = 1 + max_c.          % input width

% ---------- zeros at top of each column after gravity ----------
zero_col(C,Nz) :-
    NC = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 },
    Nz = H - NC.           % zeros that will be at the top of column C

% ---------- total number of such zeros ----------
Z = #sum { Nz : zero_col(C,Nz) }.

% we need at least one zero (Z>0) – otherwise output would be empty
:- Z > 0.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
output_rows = H * Z.
output_cols = W * Z.

% ---------- helpers for tiling ----------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < output_rows, C < output_cols.

% compute the remainder of R and C with respect to H and W
Rdiv = R / H.
R0   = R - H * Rdiv.      % R0 = R % H
Cdiv = C / W.
C0   = C - W * Cdiv.      % C0 = C % W

% ---------- gravity result for a column ----------
%  a cell of the gravity result (R0,C0) is zero iff R0 < z(C0)
output(R,C,0) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0   = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0   = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz),
    R0 < Nz.

%  otherwise we need the (R0‑z(C0)+1)-th non‑zero element of column C0

    output_cell(R,C),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0   = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0   = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz),
    R0 >= Nz,
    target = R0 - Nz + 1,
    non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N),
    N = target,
    input(Rin,C0,V),
    V != 0.

% rank of a non‑zero element inside a column (1‑based)
non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N) :-
    input(Rin,C0,V),
    V != 0,
    N = #count { R2 : input(R2,C0,V2), V2 != 0, R2 <= Rin }.

% ---------- exactly one colour per output cell ----------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Helper predicates for grid size
h_dim(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) }.
w_dim(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) }.

% Count zeros at the top of a column (columns where the top cell is zero)
zero_col(C,Nz) :-
    true,
    Nz = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V = 0 }.

% Domain of output cells (same size as the input)
output_cell(R,C) :-
    true,
    h_dim(H),
    w_dim(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% Gravity result for a cell that is empty at the top of its column
output(R,C,0) :-
    true,
    h_dim(H),
    w_dim(W),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0, R0rem < Nz.

% Gravity result for a cell that contains a non‑zero value
output(R,C,V) :-
    true,
    h_dim(H),
    w_dim(W),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    output_cell(R,C),
    V != 0,
    input(R,C,V).

% At least one column must contain a zero (otherwise the tiling would be empty)
1 { C ; Nz : zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0 } 1 :- true.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Optional helper predicates (not used elsewhere)
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% Predicates
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

calc_H(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.
calc_W(W) :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

col(C) :- C = 0..W-1.   % positive binding for column indices

% Count non-zero cells in a column
count_nonzero(C,NC) :- NC = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% For each column, compute zeros at top and zeros total
zero_col(C,Nz) :- col(C), count_nonzero(C,NC), Nz = H - NC.

% Total zeros Z
total_zeros(Z) :- true, Z = #sum { Nz : zero_col(C,Nz) }.

% Ensure at least one zero
:- total_zeros(Z), Z > 0.

% Output dimensions
calc_output_rows(OR) :- true, OR = H * Z.
calc_output_cols(OC) :- true, OC = W * Z.

output_cell(R,C) :- R >= 0, C >= 0, R < OC, C < OC.

% Define Rdiv, R0, Cdiv, C0 via arithmetic (these variables appear in rule bodies)
output(R,C,V) :- output_cell(R,C), Rdiv = R / H, R0 = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W, C0 = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz), Nz > 0, R0 < Nz,
    V = 0.
output(R,C,V) :- output_cell(R,C), Rdiv = R / H, R0 = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W, C0 = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz), R0 >= Nz,
    target = R0 - Nz + 1,
    non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N),
    N = target,
    input(Rin,C0,V),
    V != 0.

% non-zero rank
non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N) :- input(Rin,C0,V), V != 0,
    N = #count { R2 : input(R2,C0,V2), V2 != 0, R2 <= Rin }.

% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

calc_H(H) :- max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.
calc_W(W) :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

col(C) :- C = 0..W-1.

% zero count for a column
zero_col(C,Nz) :- col(C), Nz = H - #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% at least one zero

calc_output_cols(OC) :- true, OC = W * Z.

% exactly one colour per cell

max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

calc_W(W) :- max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

calc_output_cols(OC) :- true, OC = W * Z.

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity + tiling rule (fixed syntax)
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
max_row(Rmax) :- true, Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- true, Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

calc_H(H) :- true, max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.
calc_W(W) :- true, max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% ---------- column domain ----------
col(C) :- true, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- zeros at top of each column ----------
zero_col(C,Nz) :- true, col(C), Nz = H - #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ---------- total number of such zeros ----------
total_zeros(Z) :- true, Z = #sum { Nz : zero_col(C,Nz) }.

% we need at least one zero (Z>0) – otherwise output would be empty
:- total_zeros(Z), Z > 0.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
calc_output_rows(OR) :- true, OR = H * Z.
calc_output_cols(OC) :- true, OC = W * Z.

output_cell(R,C) :- true, R >= 0, C >= 0, R < OR, C < OC.

% ---------- gravity result for a column ----------

    Cdiv = C / W, C0 = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz), Nz > 0, R0 < Nz.

output(R,C,V) :- true, output_cell(R,C), Rdiv = R / H, R0 = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W, C0 = C - W * Cdiv,

    N = #count { R2 : input(R2,C0,V2), V2 != 0, R2 <= Rin }.

% ---------- exactly one colour per output cell ----------

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity + tiling rule (see the comment above)
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
max_r = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_c = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
H = 1 + max_r.          % input height
W = 1 + max_c.          % input width

% ---------- zeros at top of each column after gravity ----------
zero_col(C,Nz) :-
    NC = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 },
    Nz = H - NC.           % zeros that will be at the top of column C

% ---------- total number of such zeros ----------
Z = #sum { Nz : zero_col(C,Nz) }.

% we need at least one zero (Z>0) – otherwise output would be empty
:- Z > 0.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
output_rows = H * Z.
output_cols = W * Z.

% ---------- helpers for tiling ----------
output_cell(R,C) :-
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < output_rows, C < output_cols.

% compute the remainder of R and C with respect to H and W
Rdiv = R / H.
R0   = R - H * Rdiv.      % R0 = R % H
Cdiv = C / W.
C0   = C - W * Cdiv.      % C0 = C % W

% ---------- gravity result for a column ----------
%  a cell of the gravity result (R0,C0) is zero iff R0 < z(C0)
output(R,C,0) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0   = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0   = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz),
    R0 < Nz.

%  otherwise we need the (R0‑z(C0)+1)-th non‑zero element of column C0
output(R,C,V) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0   = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0   = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz),
    R0 >= Nz,
    target = R0 - Nz + 1,
    non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N),
    N = target,
    input(Rin,C0,V),
    V != 0.

% rank of a non‑zero element inside a column (1‑based)
non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N) :-
    input(Rin,C0,V),
    V != 0,
    N = #count { R2 : input(R2,C0,V2), V2 != 0, R2 <= Rin }.

% ---------- exactly one colour per output cell ----------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
%  Gravity + tiling rule (fixed syntax)
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
max_row(Rmax) :- true, Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- true, Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

calc_H(H) :- true, max_row(Rmax), H = 1 + Rmax.
calc_W(W) :- true, max_col(Cmax), W = 1 + Cmax.

% ---------- column domain ----------
col(C) :- true, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- zeros at top of each column ----------
zero_col(C,Nz) :- true, col(C), Nz = H - #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ---------- total number of such zeros ----------
total_zeros(Z) :- true, Z = #sum { Nz : zero_col(C,Nz) }.

% we need at least one zero (Z>0) – otherwise output would be empty
:- total_zeros(Z), Z > 0.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
calc_output_rows(OR) :- true, OR = H * Z.
calc_output_cols(OC) :- true, OC = W * Z.

output_cell(R,C) :- true, R >= 0, C >= 0, R < OR, C < OC.

% ---------- gravity result for a column ----------
output(R,C,0) :- true, output_cell(R,C), Rdiv = R / H,
    R0   = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0   = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz), Nz > 0, R0 < Nz.

output(R,C,V) :- true, output_cell(R,C), Rdiv = R / H,
    R0   = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0   = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz), R0 >= Nz,
    target = R0 - Nz + 1,
    non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N),
    N = target,
    input(Rin,C0,V),
    V != 0.

% rank of a non‑zero element inside a column (1‑based)
non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N) :- true, input(Rin,C0,V), V != 0,
    N = #count { R2 : input(R2,C0,V2), V2 != 0, R2 <= Rin }.

% ---------- exactly one colour per output cell ----------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% Gravity + tiling rule (fixed syntax)

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

calc_H(H) :- H = 1 + Rmax.

calc_W(W) :- W = 1 + Cmax.

% ---------- column domain ----------
col(C) :- C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- zeros at top of each column ----------
zero_count(C, Zc) :- Zc = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

zero_col(C, Nz) :- true, col(C), Zc = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 },
    Nz = H - Zc.

% ---------- total number of such zeros ----------
total_zeros(Z) :- Z = #sum { Nz : zero_col(C,Nz) }.

% we need at least one zero (Z>0) – otherwise output would be empty
:- total_zeros(Z), Z > 0.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
calc_output_rows(OR) :- OR = H * Z.

calc_output_cols(OC) :- OC = W * Z.

output_cell(R,C) :- true, R >= 0, C >= 0, R < OR, C < OC.

% ---------- gravity result for a column ----------
output(R,C,0) :- true, output_cell(R,C),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0 = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0 = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz), Nz > 0, R0 < Nz.

output(R,C,V) :- true, output_cell(R,C),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0 = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0 = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz), R0 >= Nz,
    target = R0 - Nz + 1,
    non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N),
    N = target,
    input(Rin,C0,V),
    V != 0.

% rank of a non‑zero element inside a column (1‑based)
non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N) :- true,
    input(Rin,C0,V), V != 0,
    N = #count { R2 : input(R2,C0,V2), V2 != 0, R2 <= Rin }.

% ---------- exactly one colour per output cell ----------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
```

calc_output_rows(OR) :- OR = H * Z.

calc_output_cols(OC) :- OC = W * Z.

output_cell(R,C) :- true, R >= 0, C >= 0, R < OR, C < OC.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% Compute dimensions inside each gravity rule
% We'll have separate rules for different cases: zero and non-zero.

% Zero result for a column
output(R,C,0) :-
    true,
    H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) },
    W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) },
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C, Nz), Nz > 0, R0rem < Nz,
    R0rem >= 0, C0rem >= 0, R0rem < H, C0rem < W.

% Non‑zero result for a cell
output(R,C,V) :-
    true,
    H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) },
    W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) },
    R0rem = R - H * (R / H),
    C0rem = C - W * (C / W),
    output_cell(R,C),   % ensure within bounds using H,W
    V != 0,
    input(R,C,V).

% Define zero column (count of zeros)
zero_col(C,Nz) :-
    true,
    Nz = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V = 0 }.

% Ensure there is at least one zero somewhere
1 { C ; Nz : zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0 } 1 :- true.

% Output cell domain (global for all cells)
output_cell(R,C) :-
    true.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Helper predicate for max row (unused but kept for completeness)
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

calc_H(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

calc_W(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
```

% Count zeros in a column
zero_col(C,Nz) :-
    true,
    Nz = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V = 0 }.

% Gravity result for a cell that is zero at top of its column
output(R,C,0) :-
    true,
    H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) },
    W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) },
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    R0rem >= 0, C0rem >= 0,
    R0rem < H, C0rem < W,
    zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0, R0rem < Nz.

% Gravity result for a non‑zero cell
output(R,C,V) :-
    true,
    H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) },
    W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) },
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    R0rem >= 0, C0rem >= 0,
    R0rem < H, C0rem < W,
    V != 0,
    input(R,C,V).

% At least one column contains a zero (otherwise the tiling would be empty)
1 { C ; Nz : zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0 } 1 :- true.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- true.

% Helper for max row (optional, not used in the main rules)
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
```

Let's draft final program:

```asp
% Zero count per column
zero_col(C,Nz) :-
    true,
    Nz = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V = 0 }.

% Predicate describing that (R,C) lies inside the output domain.
output_cell(R,C) :-
    true,
    H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) },
    W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) },
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    R0rem >= 0, C0rem >= 0,
    R0rem < H, C0rem < W.

% Gravity result for a cell that is empty (top of its column)
output(R,C,0) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0, R0rem < Nz.

% Gravity result for a cell with a non‑zero value
output(R,C,V) :-
    output_cell(R,C),
    V != 0,
    input(R,C,V).

% At least one column contains a zero (otherwise the tiling would be empty)
1 { C ; Nz : zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0 } 1 :- true.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Optional helpers (not used elsewhere)
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
```

```
% Zero count per column (columns where top cells are zero)
zero_col(C,Nz) :-
    true,
    Nz = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V = 0 }.

% Domain of output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :-
    true,
    H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) },
    W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) },
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% Gravity result for a cell that is empty at the top of its column
output(R,C,0) :-
    true,
    H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) },
    W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) },
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0, R0rem < Nz.

% Gravity result for a cell that contains a non‑zero value
output(R,C,V) :-
    true,
    H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) },
    W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) },
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    output_cell(R,C),
    V != 0,
    input(R,C,V).

% At least one column has a zero (otherwise tiling would be empty)
1 { C ; Nz : zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0 } 1 :- true.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Optional helper predicates (not used elsewhere)
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
```

% Helper predicates for grid size
h_dim(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R0 : input(R0,_,_) }.
w_dim(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C0 : input(_,C0,_) }.

% Zero count per column (columns where top cells are zero)
zero_col(C,Nz) :-
    true,
    Nz = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V = 0 }.

% Domain of output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :-
    true,
    h_dim(H),
    w_dim(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% Gravity result for a cell that is empty at the top of its column
output(R,C,0) :-
    true,
    h_dim(H),
    w_dim(W),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0, R0rem < Nz.

% Gravity result for a cell that contains a non‑zero value
output(R,C,V) :-
    true,
    h_dim(H),
    w_dim(W),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0rem = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0rem = C - W * Cdiv,
    output_cell(R,C),
    V != 0,
    input(R,C,V).

% At least one column has a zero (otherwise tiling would be empty)
1 { C ; Nz : zero_col(C,Nz), Nz > 0 } 1 :- true.

% Exactly one colour per output cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Optional helper predicates (not used elsewhere)
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
```

rounds.program_before:
% Gravity + tiling rule (fixed syntax)

% ---------- input dimensions ----------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

calc_H(H) :- H = 1 + Rmax.

calc_W(W) :- W = 1 + Cmax.

% ---------- column domain ----------
col(C) :- C = 0..W-1.

% ---------- zeros at top of each column ----------
% count non‑zero values in a column
zero_count(C, Zc) :- Zc = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% compute the number of empty cells at the top (H - non‑zero count)
zero_col(C, Nz) :- true, col(C), Zc = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 },
    Nz = H - Zc.

% ---------- total number of such zeros ----------
total_zeros(Z) :- Z = #sum { Nz : zero_col(C,Nz) }.

% we need at least one zero (Z>0) – otherwise output would be empty
:- total_zeros(Z), Z > 0.

% ---------- output dimensions ----------
calc_output_rows(OR) :- OR = H * Z.

calc_output_cols(OC) :- OC = W * Z.

output_cell(R,C) :- true, R >= 0, C >= 0, R < OR, C < OC.

% ---------- gravity result for a column ----------
output(R,C,0) :- true, output_cell(R,C),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0   = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0   = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz), Nz > 0, R0 < Nz.

output(R,C,V) :- true, output_cell(R,C),
    Rdiv = R / H,
    R0   = R - H * Rdiv,
    Cdiv = C / W,
    C0   = C - W * Cdiv,
    zero_col(C0,Nz), R0 >= Nz,
    target = R0 - Nz + 1,
    non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N),
    N = target,
    input(Rin,C0,V),
    V != 0.

% rank of a non‑zero element inside a column (1‑based)
non_zero_rank(Rin,C0,N) :- true,
    input(Rin,C0,V), V != 0,
    N = #count { R2 : input(R2,C0,V2), V2 != 0, R2 <= Rin }.

% ---------- exactly one colour per output cell ----------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% define input bounds
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width_in(Wi)  :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% output dimensions (tiling factor)
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).
width_out(WO)  :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).

% define max n for each row index R
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max { N1 : Hi * N1 <= R }.
max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max { M1 : Wi * M1 <= C }.

% compute source coordinates in input tile
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    R0 = R - Hi * N,
    R0 < Hi,
    max_m(C,M),
    C0 = C - Wi * M,
    C0 < Wi,
    input(R0, C0, V),
    V != 0.

% compute mapping existence
mapping_exists(R,C) :- #count { V : V != 0, output(R,C,V) } > 0.

% zero output only when no mapping exists
output(R,C,0) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    not mapping_exists(R,C).

% exactly one output per cell
cell(R,C) :- height_out(HO), width_out(WO), R >= 0, C >= 0, R < HO, C < WO.

:- 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ensure color domain
color(0..9).

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.
%    After gravity the pattern of colours forms a compact rectangle.
% 2. Tiling – this rectangle is copied repeatedly to the right and down.
%    The number of copies in each direction equals (height+1) and
%    (width+1) where *height* and *width* are the dimensions of the
%    gravity‑rectangle (i.e. the original input size after gravity).
%    Thus the final output grid has size 
%          height_out = height * (height+1)
%          width_out  = width  * (width+1)
%    and each output cell (R,C) receives the colour of the input cell
%    at ( (R mod height), (C mod width) ).  Cells that receive no
%    colour are filled with 0.
%
% The program below implements exactly this rule using ASP‑Clingo.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width_in(Wi)  :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).               % vertical copies
width_out (WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).               % horizontal copies

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal number of whole tiles that fit -----
% For a row index R we need the greatest whole‑tile count N such that
%   Hi * N <= R .  The remainder R0 = R - Hi*N is the column index
%   inside the original tile (0 … Hi‑1).  The same is done for columns.
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max { N1 : Hi * N1 <= R }.
max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max { M1 : Wi * M1 <= C }.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    R0 = R - Hi * N,      R0 < Hi,
    max_m(C,M),
    C0 = C - Wi * M,      C0 < Wi,
    input(R0, C0, V),     V != 0.                     % colour from tile

% ----------- 5. Detect where a non‑zero colour exists --------------
% M > 0  ⇔ there is at least one non‑zero colour at (R,C)
mapping_exists(R,C) :-
    M = #count { V : output(R,C,V), V != 0 },
    M > 0.

% ----------- 6. Fill the background with 0 only when no colour exists
output(R,C,0) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    not mapping_exists(R,C).

% ----------- 7. Grid cells (the domain of output) --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

% ----------- 8. Exactly one colour per output cell -----------------
% colour(0..9) is supplied by the harness.
:- 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% The program yields one answer set per training example that
% reproduces the expected output grids.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
```

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width_in(Wi)  :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).               % vertical copies
width_out (WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).               % horizontal copies

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal number of whole tiles that fit -----
% For a row index R we need the greatest whole‑tile count N such that
%   Hi * N <= R .  The remainder R0 = R - Hi*N is the column index
%   inside the original tile (0 … Hi‑1).  The same is done for columns.
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max { N1 : Hi * N1 <= R }.
max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max { M1 : Wi * M1 <= C }.

output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.
%    After gravity the pattern of colours forms a compact rectangle.
% 2. Tiling – this rectangle is copied repeatedly to the right and down.
%    The number of copies in each direction equals (height+1) and
%    (width+1) where *height* and *width* are the dimensions of the
%    gravity‑rectangle (i.e. the original input size after gravity).
%    Thus the final output grid has size 
%          height_out = height * (height+1)
%          width_out  = width * (width+1)
%    and each output cell (R,C) receives the colour of the input cell
%    at ( (R mod height), (C mod width) ).  Cells that receive no
%    colour are filled with 0.
%
% The program below implements exactly this rule using ASP‑Clingo.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width_in(Wi) :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).

width_out(WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal whole tiles fitting into a row -----
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max { N1 : (Hi * N1) <= R }.

max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max { M1 : (Wi * M1) <= C }.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    max_m(C,M),
    input((R - Hi * N), (C - Wi * M), V), V != 0.

% ----------- 5. Detect cells that have at least one coloured tile ---
has_color(R,C) :- M = #count { V : output(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ----------- 6. Background rule (zero colour when no colour exists) ---
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).

% ----------- 7. Grid cells (the domain of output) --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

% ----------- 8. Exactly one colour per output cell -----------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% The program yields one answer set per training example that
% reproduces the expected output grids.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.
%    After gravity the pattern of colours forms a compact rectangle.
% 2. Tiling – this rectangle is copied repeatedly to the right and down.
%    The number of copies in each direction equals (height+1) and
%    (width+1) where *height* and *width* are the dimensions of the
%    gravity‑rectangle (i.e. the original input size after gravity).
%    Thus the final output grid has size 
%          height_out = height * (height+1)
%          width_out  = width * (width+1)
%    and each output cell (R,C) receives the colour of the input cell
%    at ( (R mod height), (C mod width) ).  Cells that receive no
%    colour are filled with 0.
%
% The program below implements exactly this rule using ASP‑Clingo.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width_in(Wi)  :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).               % vertical copies
width_out(WO)  :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).               % horizontal copies

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal whole tiles fitting into a row -----
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max { N1 : Hi * N1 <= R }.
max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max { M1 : Wi * M1 <= C }.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    max_m(C,M),
    input(R - Hi * N, C - Wi * M, V), V != 0.

% ----------- 5. Detect cells that have at least one coloured tile ---
has_color(R,C) :- M = #count { V : output(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ----------- 6. Background rule (zero colour when no colour exists) ---
output(R,C,0) :-
    cell(R,C),
    not has_color(R,C).

% ----------- 7. Grid cells (the domain of output) --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

% ----------- 8. Exactly one colour per output cell -----------------
% colour(0..9) is supplied by the harness.
:- 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% The program yields one answer set per training example that
% reproduces the expected output grids.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
```

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.
%    After gravity the pattern of colours forms a compact rectangle.
% 2. Tiling – this rectangle is copied repeatedly to the right and down.
%    The number of copies in each direction equals (height+1) and
%    (width+1) where *height* and *width* are the dimensions of the
%    gravity‑rectangle (i.e. the original input size after gravity).
%    Thus the final output grid has size 
%          height_out = height * (height+1)
%          width_out  = width * (width+1)
%    and each output cell (R,C) receives the colour of the input cell
%    at ( (R mod height), (C mod width) ).  Cells that receive no
%    colour are filled with 0.
%
% The program below implements exactly this rule using ASP‑Clingo.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width_in(Wi) :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).               % vertical copies

width_out(WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).               % horizontal copies

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal whole tiles fitting into a row -----
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max { N1 : Hi * N1 <= R }.

max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max { M1 : Wi * M1 <= C }.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    max_m(C,M),
    input(R - Hi * N, C - Wi * M, V), V != 0.

% ----------- 5. Detect cells that have at least one coloured tile ---
has_color(R,C) :- M = #count { V : output(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ----------- 6. Background rule (zero colour when no colour exists) ---
output(R,C,0) :-
    cell(R,C),
    not has_color(R,C).

% ----------- 7. Grid cells (the domain of output) --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

% ----------- 8. Exactly one colour per output cell -----------------
% colour(0..9) is supplied by the harness.
:- 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% The program yields one answer set per training example that
% reproduces the expected output grids.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

rounds.response:
```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.
%    After gravity the pattern of colours forms a compact rectangle.
% 2. Tiling – this rectangle is copied repeatedly to the right and down.
%    The number of copies in each direction equals (height+1) and
%    (width+1) where *height* and *width* are the dimensions of the
%    gravity‑rectangle (i.e. the original input size after gravity).
%    Thus the final output grid has size 
%          height_out = height * (height+1)
%          width_out  = width * (width+1)
%    and each output cell (R,C) receives the colour of the input cell
%    at ( (R mod height), (C mod width) ).  Cells that receive no
%    colour are filled with 0.
%
% The program below implements exactly this rule using ASP‑Clingo.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width_in(Wi) :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).               % vertical copies

width_out(WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).               % horizontal copies

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal whole tiles fitting into a row -----
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max { N1 : Hi * N1 <= R }.

max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max { M1 : Wi * M1 <= C }.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    max_m(C,M),
    input(R - Hi * N, C - Wi * M, V), V != 0.

% ----------- 5. Detect cells that have at least one coloured tile ---
has_color(R,C) :- M = #count { V : output(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ----------- 6. Background rule (zero colour when no colour exists) ---
output(R,C,0) :-
    cell(R,C),
    not has_color(R,C).

% ----------- 7. Grid cells (the domain of output) --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

% ----------- 8. Exactly one colour per output cell -----------------
% colour(0..9) is supplied by the harness.
:- 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% The program yields one answer set per training example that
% reproduces the expected output grids.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
```

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.
%    After gravity the pattern of colours forms a compact rectangle.
% 2. Tiling – this rectangle is copied repeatedly to the right and down.
%    The number of copies in each direction equals (height+1) and
%    (width+1) where *height* and *width* are the dimensions of the
%    gravity‑rectangle (i.e. the original input size after gravity).
%    Thus the final output grid has size 
%          height_out = height * (height+1)
%          width_out  = width  * (width+1)
%    and each output cell (R,C) receives the colour of the input cell
%    at ( (R mod height), (C mod width) ).  Cells that receive no
%    colour are filled with 0.
%
% The program below implements exactly this rule using ASP‑Clingo.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width_in(Wi)  :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).               % vertical copies
width_out (WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).               % horizontal copies

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal number of whole tiles that fit -----
% For a row index R we need the greatest whole‑tile count N such that
%   Hi * N <= R .  The remainder R0 = R - Hi*N is the column index
%   inside the original tile (0 … Hi‑1).  The same is done for columns.
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max { N1 : Hi * N1 <= R }.
max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max { M1 : Wi * M1 <= C }.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    R0 = R - Hi * N,      R0 < Hi,
    max_m(C,M),
    C0 = C - Wi * M,      C0 < Wi,
    input(R0, C0, V),     V != 0.                     % colour from tile

% ----------- 5. Detect where a non‑zero colour exists --------------
% M > 0  ⇔ there is at least one non‑zero colour at (R,C)
mapping_exists(R,C) :-
    M = #count { V : output(R,C,V), V != 0 },
    M > 0.

% ----------- 6. Fill the background with 0 only when no colour exists
output(R,C,0) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    not mapping_exists(R,C).

% ----------- 7. Grid cells (the domain of output) --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

% ----------- 8. Exactly one colour per output cell -----------------
% colour(0..9) is supplied by the harness.
:- 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% The program yields one answer set per training example that
% reproduces the expected output grids.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.
%    After gravity the pattern of colours forms a compact rectangle.
% 2. Tiling – this rectangle is copied repeatedly to the right and down.
%    The number of copies in each direction equals (height+1) and
%    (width+1) where *height* and *width* are the dimensions of the
%    gravity‑rectangle (i.e. the original input size after gravity).
%    Thus the final output grid has size 
%          height_out = height * (height+1)
%          width_out  = width * (width+1)
%    and each output cell (R,C) receives the colour of the input cell
%    at ( (R mod height), (C mod width) ).  Cells that receive no
%    colour are filled with 0.
%
% The program below implements exactly this rule using ASP‑Clingo.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max{R : input(R,_,_)}.
width_in(Wi) :- Wi = 1 + #max{C : input(_,C,_)}.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).
width_out(WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal whole tiles fitting into a row -----
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max{N1 : (Hi * N1) <= R }.
max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max{M1 : (Wi * M1) <= C }.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    max_m(C,M),
    input((R - Hi * N), (C - Wi * M), V), V != 0.

% ----------- 5. Detect cells that have at least one coloured tile ---
has_color(R,C) :- M = #count{V : output(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ----------- 6. Background rule (zero colour when no colour exists) ---
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).

% ----------- 7. Grid cells (the domain of output) --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

% ----------- 8. Exactly one colour per output cell -----------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% The program yields one answer set per training example that
% reproduces the expected output grids.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
```

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.
%    After gravity the pattern of colours forms a compact rectangle.
% 2. Tiling – this rectangle is copied repeatedly to the right and down.
%    The number of copies in each direction equals (height+1) and
%    (width+1) where *height* and *width* are the dimensions of the
%    gravity‑rectangle (i.e. the original input size after gravity).
%    Thus the final output grid has size 
%          height_out = height * (height+1)
%          width_out  = width * (width+1)
%    and each output cell (R,C) receives the colour of the input cell
%    at ( (R mod height), (C mod width) ).  Cells that receive no
%    colour are filled with 0.
%
% The program below implements exactly this rule using ASP‑Clingo.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max{R : input(R,_,_)}.
width_in(Wi) :- Wi = 1 + #max{C : input(_,C,_)}.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).
width_out(WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal whole tiles fitting into a row -----
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max{N1 : (Hi * N1) <= R}.
max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max{M1 : (Wi * M1) <= C}.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    max_m(C,M),
    input((R - Hi * N), (C - Wi * M), V), V != 0.

% ----------- 5. Detect cells that have at least one coloured tile ---
has_color(R,C) :- M = #count{V : output(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% ----------- 6. Background rule (zero colour when no colour exists) ---
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).

% ----------- 7. Grid cells (the domain of output) --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

% ----------- 8. Exactly one colour per output cell -----------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% The program yields one answer set per training example that
% reproduces the expected output grids.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

rounds.response:
```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Gravity – in each column all non‑zero cells “fall” to the bottom.
%    After gravity the pattern of colours forms a compact rectangle.
% 2. Tiling – this rectangle is copied repeatedly to the right and down.
%    The number of copies in each direction equals (height+1) and
%    (width+1) where *height* and *width* are the dimensions of the
%    gravity‑rectangle (i.e. the original input size after gravity).
%    Thus the final output grid has size 
%          height_out = height * (height+1)
%          width_out  = width * (width+1)
%    and each output cell (R,C) receives the colour of the input cell
%    at ( (R mod height), (C mod width) ).  Cells that receive no
%    colour are filled with 0.
%
% The program below implements exactly this rule using ASP‑Clingo.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------- 1. Input dimensions ------------------------------------
height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max{R : input(R,_,_)}.
width_in(Wi) :- Wi = 1 + #max{C : input(_,C,_)}.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).
width_out(WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).

% ----------- 3. Helper: maximal whole tiles fitting into a row -----
max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max{N1 : (Hi * N1) <= R}.
max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max{M1 : (Wi * M1) <= C}.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    max_m(C,M),
    input((R - Hi * N), (C - Wi * M), V), V != 0.

% ----------- 5. Detect cells that have at least one coloured tile ---
has_color(R,C) :- M = #count{V : output(R,C,V), V != 0}.

% ----------- 6. Background rule (zero colour when no colour exists) ---
output(R,C,0) :- cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).

% ----------- 7. Grid cells (the domain of output) --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

% ----------- 8. Exactly one colour per output cell -----------------
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% The program yields one answer set per training example that
% reproduces the expected output grids.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
```

height_in(Hi) :- Hi = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width_in(Wi) :- Wi = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ----------- 2. Output dimensions (tiling factor) -------------------
height_out(HO) :- HO = Hi * (Hi + 1).               % vertical copies

width_out(WO) :- WO = Wi * (Wi + 1).               % horizontal copies

max_n(R,N) :- R >= 0, R < HO, N = #max { N1 : Hi * N1 <= R }.

max_m(C,M) :- C >= 0, C < WO, M = #max { M1 : Wi * M1 <= C }.

% ----------- 4. Direct derivation: colour from the tiled tile -------
output(R,C,V) :-
    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO,
    max_n(R,N),
    max_m(C,M),
    input(R - Hi * N, C - Wi * M, V), V != 0.

has_color(R,C) :- M = #count { V : output(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

    cell(R,C),
    not has_color(R,C).

    height_out(HO), width_out(WO),
    R >= 0, C >= 0,
    R < HO, C < WO.

:- 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
